title: Federal Government Nationalizes Tren Suburbano for 6B Pesos; Buenavista–AIFA Express Renamed 'Felipe Ángeles' and to Launch April 26
detail: At her April 23 mañanera, President Sheinbaum announced the federal government had completed the acquisition of private stakes in the Suburban Train (Tren Suburbano), buying out Spain's Construcciones y Auxiliar de Ferrocarriles (CAF) and Omnitren for 5,999 million pesos (approximately USD $309 million). The acquisition is financed through FONADIN (National Infrastructure Fund), placing full operational management of the Buenavista–Felipe Ángeles International Airport (AIFA) corridor under state control. Jorge Mendoza, director of Banobras (Mexico's national development bank), presented the acquisition details at the press conference. Sheinbaum confirmed the inaugural run on Sunday, April 26, 2026 — less than two months before the FIFA World Cup 2026 kickoff (June 11) — giving Mexico City a direct 40-minute rail link from the historic Buenavista station in the city center to AIFA. The 23.7-kilometer line extends an existing Buenavista–Cuautitlán suburban rail line with a new branch running northeast to AIFA; the formal name for the airport express is 'Tren Felipe Ángeles.' The nationalization continues Sheinbaum's infrastructure consolidation agenda: FONADIN now controls the suburban rail corridor alongside the Maya Train, the Transistmic Corridor, and airport infrastructure. AIFA, built during AMLO's presidency, has struggled to attract passenger volume since opening; the direct rail link is expected to substantially boost ridership and relieve road congestion during the World Cup. CAF is the Spanish rail engineering conglomerate that previously held the operational concession. Some private sector voices flagged that the government is absorbing estimated annual operating losses of several hundred million pesos, but the administration framed the purchase as essential World Cup–era connectivity infrastructure.

media.caption: Sheinbaum announces federal takeover of Tren Suburbano at April 23 mañanera; AIFA express renamed 'Tren Felipe Ángeles'

title: Sheinbaum Formally Confirms Luisa María Alcalde as Legal Counsel; She Leaves Morena Presidency Effective May 1
detail: President Sheinbaum confirmed at the April 23 mañanera that Luisa María Alcalde Luján will formally assume the role of Legal Counsel to the Executive (Consejera Jurídica del Ejecutivo Federal) starting May 1, 2026. Alcalde had been invited to the role at the April 22 press conference but had asked for a brief pause ('un ratito') before accepting — unusual in a tightly managed cabinet structure. She replaces Esthela Damián Peralta, who stepped down to compete for a congressional candidacy in Guerrero in the 2027 midterm cycle. Alcalde brings formidable political and legal weight to the presidential legal office: under AMLO she served as Labor Secretary (2018–2022) and then Interior Secretary (SEGOB, 2022–2024), where she oversaw domestic security policy. Under Sheinbaum, she was elected Morena National President in October 2024 — replacing Mario Delgado who became SEP Secretary — and grew Morena party membership from under 3 million to over 12 million affiliates. Sheinbaum praised her legal background and her 'excellent' work leading Morena. Critics noted that Alcalde lacks traditional private-sector or judicial law expertise, and some interpreted the appointment as Sheinbaum consolidating a loyalist in a sensitive role that oversees the executive branch's legal strategy — including coordination with the newly elected Morena-aligned Supreme Court on pending constitutional litigation. The transition also opens the Morena party presidency to competition: candidates for the leadership must emerge before the 2027 midterm electoral season accelerates.

media.caption: Luisa María Alcalde confirmed as Consejera Jurídica del Ejecutivo Federal, effective May 1, leaving the Morena national presidency

title: Mexico Sends Formal Diplomatic Note to U.S. Ambassador Over Unauthorized CIA Operation in Chihuahua; Sheinbaum Weighs Sanctions on State Government
detail: At the April 23 mañanera, President Sheinbaum provided the most detailed official account to date of the sovereignty crisis triggered by the unauthorized U.S. CIA operation in Chihuahua. On April 20, two CIA agents (described publicly by U.S. officials as 'Embassy officials') and two Mexican Agencia Estatal de Investigación (AEI) officers were killed when their vehicle crashed while returning from an unauthorized joint drug-lab demolition in Chihuahua state. The Mexican Army (SEDENA) and federal security forces (SSPC) were both unaware of the operation, which was arranged between U.S. CIA operatives and Chihuahua state investigators without federal authorization. Sheinbaum confirmed Mexico formally delivered a diplomatic note to U.S. Ambassador Ken Salazar: 'There cannot be agents from any U.S. government institution operating in the Mexican field without federal authorization.' She indicated her government is reviewing whether Chihuahua Governor Maru Campos or other state officials violated federal law by hosting CIA agents without notifying Mexico City — a potentially sanctionable offense. 'This is not a minor matter,' Sheinbaum said. U.S. and Mexican officials have given divergent accounts: Washington characterizes it as a legitimate joint anti-drug operation; Mexico insists the federal government was bypassed and its sovereignty violated. The incident reinvigorates Sheinbaum's proposed constitutional reform to codify prohibitions on unauthorized foreign agent activity on Mexican soil. Sheinbaum separately reiterated that the April 16 authorization of 23 U.S. Navy SEAL Team 8 members for a constitutional joint training exercise followed proper federal procedures — explicitly distinguishing it from the Chihuahua CIA operation to avoid conflating authorized with unauthorized cooperation.

media.caption: President Sheinbaum at press conference addressing the CIA Chihuahua incident and Mexico's formal diplomatic response
